sentences.text: Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I arrived to the town of Papa.
My geography, and [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE] with-- with this truck, which was going for supplies and so on, apparently helped me.
I didn't walk much after that.
And I got on a train, which was not a scheduled train, but some train which I understood is going to Budapest.

And on that train, and with supply of some food which from that storage area, the Russian soldiers let me have some bread, and some dried meat, and some can of water, and so forth, I was on the train.
And I wasn't alone.
There were town people from the neighborhood areas and so on.
And the train had all kind of makeshift arrangements, some of it was seating.
Some it was-- some of the people even have tickets.
And I had, by the way, some Hungarian money.
Because one of the Russian soldier reached into his pocket and gave me a bunch of money, which I had no idea.
It looked different from the money which I used to see.
But it was written in Hungarian on it.
So it was some new money they printed, because the city of Budapest, and before they got to the border, a considerable time passed by.
And by then, there was some sort of government in Budapest, which we started to print money.
And life started to return apparently.
So with that money, I thought, if you need ticket, I can buy ticket.
But then I-- I heard that the whistle of the train is about to give signal that they take off, so a bunch of us were sitting on the top of wagons, just out on the top of the wagon, sitting on it.
And the train took off and I was sitting on the top.
And there were other people, peasants, from nearby towns and so on.
And they had plenty food.
They opened up their food, a-- a-- a little towel which was tied in knots, and opened up.
And everybody shared food and so on.
And in about a day and a half, I had arrived to Budapest, just very close to a sports stadium which was called the [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH].
English translation would be the sports stadium.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] France, which is [NON-ENGLISH] in German, or in English, I suddenly don't have the English name for it.
And I knew the location.
I knew that what street is it on.
So I just started to walk, and walk, and walk, and trying to get back to my own neighborhood and find out what's left of it.
The city looked like that there were some scars all over, because the buildings had all kind of signs on the cover of the buildings, on the plaster of the buildings which were gunshot wounds.
And many buildings were in despair and burned out.
But the majority of the city, from what I see from the suburbs as I was moving, was recognizable and well.
As I found out later, there was not one single bridge on the Danube which was intact.

And you couldn't cross from one side of the city to the other, just like here in the Potomac River dividing us from Washington DC to Virginia, and all these wonderful bridges, that would be none of them existing.
It was some six, seven bridges of Budapest, which was all blown up by the Germans as they retreated to slow down the Russian movement.
I was in the border in the concentration camp--
--when this happened.
I seen the ruins of the bridges.

Yes, and I was able to see that so-called pontoon bridge, which was portions of wooded floats, which re-established the first closure between the two sides of the city, the hilly of Buda and the [INAUDIBLE] side, which was the major commercial and apartment building side.
And so I arrived to the neighborhood where I was.
And before we were taken to the Jewish house, I was on my way seeing that building, which was bombed out.
The first two floors of the building was shattered.
And the lower first floor was partly there.
And nobody lived in the building.
No, that was the house which before the Jewish house.
And a few blocks away, I went to the Jewish house.
It had no more the star of David on the front door.
That was taken off.
And I went to the attendant's house, apartment on the first floor.
And the attendant's door, I was knocking on.
And a woman whom I remembered well, as far as her looks, I knew that she is the wife of the attendant over the building.
And she said, what do you want?
Looking at me kind of strangely, I must have been some kind of a little unusual sight of a skin-and-bone little creature and all kind of raggedy clothing on.
And I said, I am inquiring if anybody here from the days when this was a Jewish house is still alive and here.
And she looks at me, yes, there are some people here.
Whom are you looking for?
And I said, I'm looking for the Lichtmanns.
And my name is-- and she says, you are, I can tell now, you are.
Your father is here.
[SOB] So I moved back to the portion of the courtyard. , And the first floor I was knocking on the door.
And I heard a noise inside that people are there.
Nobody came to the work.
So I knocked the door again.
Nobody came.
And I was persistent, knocking on the door.
I was kicking it a little too, to make a little louder noise where they must hear me.
Then the door opens up a little bit.
And somebody says, whom do you want?
What do you want?
And I said, Mr.
Lichtmann, my father.
And that person shrieks out, says Mr.
Lichtmann, your son is here.
Imagine.
I was asking, why didn't they let me in?
Why did they not answer the door?

Because the fear was the Russians, the occupying Russian force of the city, were rounding up people for free labor, and whatever they needed them for, either to help them with the-- with the locations, or-- or to try to clean up city, or whatever.
And people were not willing to open doors.
So finally, I came in.
And then obviously, my father hugging me and so on.
And just take off your clothes and put something decent on.
We can find something for you.
And no longer than an hour, and I gave my father, I says, I have some money here.
And then I obviously was asking my mother.
And father said, we'll talk about it.
We don't know about her where being.
We don't know a thing.
And it was fairly early in the morning and father says, I'll be back.
I am going to the temple.
You just lie down here.
So father went to the temple.
And he was given his thanks to God.
And he says, I donated portion of the money for the poor, which you had with you.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember those words very well.
So then he said, what we need now is, you have to be seen by a doctor.
And so it was.
I was seen by a doctor, who was trying to tell what not to do, what to do, and so on.
And no matter how I was trying to follow instructions, not to eat a lot suddenly and so on, I suddenly gained too much weight.
I was very blown up.
And the skin on my body, which was shriveled, especially in joints like my knee and my elbow, stretched out.
And it was kind of watery under the freshly stretched-out skin and so on.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I gained back strength slowly and so on, and life begun, the free life begun.
We were always very respectful of the Russians on the street, where the Russian occupying force was present in Budapest, because they were catching everybody and anybody.

And when I said that, catching everybody and anybody, I have to come back to an incident on my walk from Harka, on the road after-- before-- before I experienced getting this transportation which I was talking about.
There was a portion I was across with a bunch of bicycles, which were left on the roadside.
And I had a love for bicycles as a child.
I was a 16-year-old, 15-year-old, very much involved.

And when I could go to the stadium at age 14-- and later on, you couldn't enter the stadium and so on-- I was part of a team for a bicycle race group who were assembling bicycles and changing wheels and so forth.
So I understood the mechanics of a bicycle.
And out of several bicycles, I put together a bicycle which was functioning.
And I was going for a mile or so before this event happened with the Russians tanks and so on.

And I also encountered on-- on the trip with the people who were Russians on that truck who were taking me to whatever location they were going, a-- a surrounded area where Russians were having behind some makeshift fences, all kind of people.
And the truck stopped there, and I was walking around.
And from inside, some people shouted at me, get away, because they round you up.
The Russians are producing here a certain number of people whom they need to transport back to Russia.
And they round up anybody and everybody for God knows what reasons.
So you-- you just get away.
So I just went back to my little Russian truck.
And-- and I was pre-warned what happens.

And this was indeed truth, because many years later, many years later, I was a physician by then, worked in Budapest in a hospital, where a doctor who joined our staff was somebody who was member of the-- well, he was on the Russian front with the forced labor units.
Then he was in a concentration camp somewhere on the Austrian border.
And then when he was freed by the Russians, he was rounded up by some other Russians, taken to Siberia.
And it took him two years to be freed and returned to Hungary.
In the meantime, that was Dr.
Krausz, Joszef, Joseph Krausz, who told us that story.
And when he came home, he was looking for his wife, whom he found.
But after two years, she remarried, not believing that her husband could be still alive.
So strange stories happened in this-- in this misery, and war-torn, and-- and-- and-- and-- and suffering population of surviving Jews, as well as anybody who survives a war.
How did we find out-- found out about my mother?
As I mentioned, life started to return and normalize in the city.
And my father was starting to, again, establish himself in business.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was always going, he said, I'm going into a portion of the city for a newspaper's [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] editorial location, where the paper is edited.
And outside on the fence, under glass, there is always a list of people who are known to be alive from different concentrations camp, who survived.
By then, there was obvious knowledge that many people were taken to the concentration camps and started to have people like myself who returned to tell the world what happened and where were we.

And before I tell about how we found out about my mother, I have to stop and to tell about a very sad moment when my little friend, Tomas Stern's father and mother who survived, were asking what do I know about their son.
And I had to deliver the news that I know he is dead.
And I-- I tell you the feeling, which I had at the time, to face somebody to tell.
I felt guilty.
I still feel guilty.
Because somehow, why didn't he survived with me?
He should have.
I was luckier.
And whenever I walked in the street, on the same street where the parents of him lived, I was, whenever I notice from a distance, I was trying to avoid to face them again.
Because it was very painful to me, that I made it.
It's guilt feeling.
And I'm sure I'm not the only one who delivered news like this on return.
And then as time went by, and one is jumping back and forth what was life like, it came to news that some people still survived the next death march, and then put it into wagons.

When the-- when the-- when the people who were evacuated from Harka and the other locations which were nearby, as I found out, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and many others in that neighborhood, they were all then herded away and taken to [NON-ENGLISH] and Mauthausen, places where very few survived.
And many of those names or a couple of those names whom I mentioned, this Edelstein, he had a very Hebrew first name.
They called him Edelstein, Alter Leiben.
It was a Yiddish German name.
And Lobo, the name, I never-- maybe they survived, but I never found out in the city of Budapest if they did.
And then [NON-ENGLISH] and Mauthausen, were later on to be found.
They're very infamous camps where the massacre of thousands and thousands did occur.
So that leads to tell that we still didn't have my mother.
My grandmother survived in the Ghetto.
My father survived in the Ghetto, Budapest Ghetto.
Ghetto is not an establishment in Hungary which was there before the Nazi times.
Some towns, and you hear stories about life in middle ages where Jews lived in ghettos.
But that ghetto was created in the central part of the city, which was heavily Jewishly populated, a certain area where some 80,000, 90,000 Jews were concentrated.
And the fate of the Jews was that they either planned on taking them out to a concentration camp and extermination camp locations, or-- or to blow up and then finish up everybody who is alive there.
And life wasn't easy there because food was very scarce.
Disease in the ghetto was rampant.
And many people will die, even so if they lived under as confined area of the city.
But for about six weeks or so, there were just decimated by death and disease, because there was no medical help.
Older people, basic medical needs were not available and so on, except some Jewish physicians who were trying their best for whatever supplies they could have.
And they were having some contact with the outside world.
And bribe and money somehow sometime let some medical supplies in.
And I am not the one to tell about Ghetto life.
All what I know about is what my father told me.
And we didn't know still anything about my mother.
And I found my grandmother to be alive.
And I found my mother's sister in the city who were alive.
Pardon me.
My grandmother, well, on the papers you asked me to fill out, we had to give the maiden names.
Well, maiden name was Rosa Friedmann.
And her married name was Schleyer, like my mother's maiden name and some of the names I mentioned.
And-- and I do know for a fact that Viktor Schleyer, my mother's brother, his wife, his two daughters, and the older daughter's child, her husband, were all taken to Auschwitz-- never made it.
I do have cousins.
I wrote down names, but I don't know if you should list the names one by one here, whom I remember from the family who did not make it.
And-- and I have an uncle by the name of Laszlo Lichtmann, who my father's brother-- half brother because father's mother died in childbirth-- didn't make it from concentration camp.
Father's sister, Margit, didn't make it-- died in concentration camp.
We don't know exactly who died where, which one.
Sometimes, some few survivors could be contacted, and they said that possible from that location.
Many of these relatives didn't live in Budapest.
They were less fortunate.
The city of Budapest, even so if I was taken away, even so if my mother, which I didn't speak much about yet, because we didn't know what her fate was, was taken away.
So the Budapest Jews were not so heavily victimized by the final solution of the Third Reich Germany.
And talking about mother, one day, Father comes home and says, I can't tell you how much hope we have for mother being alive.
In that newspaper's fence where the list of people are put out, I read the name of Ida Schleyer.
I read the birth place, which is [NON-ENGLISH].
And I read the year of birth, which wasn't hers.
It was a number of years younger than hers.
But if she would have known another Ida Schleyer in [NON-ENGLISH], she would have talked about it.
So I think for whatever reason, either they misprinted it, but there is hope that it is she who-- whose name I found.
And according that list, the International Red Cross is publishing the list, she is in Sweden.
So how to contact the International Red Cross in this war-torn country which starts to emerge.
And communications with Sweden are not necessarily the best.
And who are the Lichtmanns to find out and so forth.
But we tried to contact Red Cross and so on, and tell it to them.
And a week went by, and we were just living on hope.
And another 10 days went by, or a week went by, and we were living on the hope that somebody will answer and so on.
And father gave our address.
And we didn't live in the same location as at that time, and so forth, which is too long to go into on how it worked.

And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a person who lived in the same building where I lived until I was about from age 9-- age 8, age 9, until around age 14, when we had to leave it, because Jews were not permitted to continue in that job where my father and mother was to be an attendant in an apartment building.
There was a shoemaker, Mr.
Molina.
I remember his name.
And I spent an awful lot of time with Mr.

Molina because, as a child, I admired looking at as he was sitting on his little stool, and in his lap and his apron, he had the shoes which he either fixed or made a new, shoe and had all these wooden pegs in his mouth, and so expertly was spitting them out, one after the other, and hammering them in, and making the shoes.
And he always had to sit there doing his work.
And he was always kind to talk to me when I was looking for somebody to talk to as a little boy.
And then Mr.
Molina was the one who came forward, and telephone communications in the city were almost not for private [INAUDIBLE], just like before the war.
Private people didn't have too many telephones, if any.
And he is knocking on our door where he found us.
He knew that we were living in the neighborhood again, but not in the location where the building was.
And my father, after the war, made contact with him and so on, or maybe wanted to get his shoes fixed.
[LAUGHS] So he says, Mr.
Lichtmann-- and he knew I was already back-- he says your wife, your mother wrote a letter from Sweden to us, Mr.
Molina, care of.
And in the letter, he's asking, he wants-- she wants to know if you people are alive or if I would know anything about it.
He was hoping that this contact might clear her curiosity, and then hopes that you are, if I know anything about you.
So why don't you answer?
Why don't you answer?
And we did.
And in a couple of weeks, we got a letter to our address.
And mail between countries started to develop.
And I returned to finish high school education, which is another story again.
And mother said that she's in Sweden.
The Swedish [INAUDIBLE] took her from Bergen-Belsen She was in the famous, infamous Bergen-Belsen concentration camp.
She was turned away from Auschwitz.
Auschwitz they were almost at the gates of Auschwitz, and Auschwitz didn't take them.
And they were turned off and taken to Bergen-Belsen.
And that's where she survived in terribly, miserable physical shape.
And then in Sweden, the Swedish [INAUDIBLE] placed her in a hospital and built her up.
And she was already having a job in Sweden and working.
We are talking about now probably somewhere around August of that year when I was freed in April.
That was already August, September, when we found out that mother is alive.
And we started to correspond.
And I can tell you the gladness and then the hope.
And I have to emphasize that I felt from then on that I am somewhat of an absolutely fortunate individual, because we had only me and my parents, and all three of us survived in a different location.
And hardly anybody in our own family is intact like the three of us.
And I started to list people, like my uncle who lived in that town [NON-ENGLISH], and his wife and his daughter-- Auschwitz.

I mentioned maybe a doctor, not necessarily while you are taping this witness testimony, but maybe in our conversation before, that there was a doctor in [NON-ENGLISH] who was my grandmother's brother, who was a physician who practiced medicine in the United States.
And because of marital difficulties, he returned to Hungary.
And he was taken to the concentration camp at his age of well in his 70s.
So was his wife, who taught me while on summer vacation at age 6 and 7 to read Hebrew.
She was a Hebrew teacher.
And-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] both sides of the family and the extended family-- second cousins, uncles-- they were very, very much affected.
There was hardly any family who was intact like the three of us.
I can't tell you that-- what a-- what a feeling it is to be alive and-- and-- and-- and building a new family, my own, which I am so proud and so happy with.
And then when I returned to school, and maybe we should have a little moment that I collect myself to tell you what happened to my schoolmates.
